Vibhaa Sharma

Bachelor of Engineering (Honours) in Engineering Science

Testing & Systems Engineering — Auckland Programme for Space Systems

Assembling a test and flight electron emitter PCB

What: Assembled both test and flight versions of an electron emitter payload for an aerospace project.

How: Followed detailed assembly documentation, performed soldering and inspections, and used test jigs and verification equipment to validate functionality.

Results: Produced reliable flight-ready hardware while gaining practical experience in electronics assembly, testing, and quality assurance processes.

Integration testing method and documentation of a radiation payload with cobalt-60

What: Developed integration test documentation for a radiation-detection payload.

How: Translated system requirements into structured test procedures and worked with the software team to define test cases and verification methods for testing with Cobalt-60 radioactive sources.

Results: Created test documentation that will be used by the Physics Department to conduct formal payload testing and performance evaluation.

Thermal qualification test documentation

What: Contributed to qualification testing activities for aerospace payload systems.

How: Developed and reviewed test procedures based on NASA GEVS requirements, helping define environmental and functional verification tests.

Results: Supported the preparation of qualification testing campaigns and improved understanding of aerospace verification, validation, and environmental testing standards.

Rocketry Club Level 1 Rocket Build

Analysis and simulations

I conducted extensive flight simulations using OpenRocket to evaluate the performance of multiple rocket configurations. Parameters such as fin geometry, mass distribution, motor selection, and were varied to assess their impact on apogee, stability margin, velocity, and recovery performance. This process improved my understanding of rocket aerodynamics, design optimisation, and the use of simulation tools to support engineering decision-making.

Fins analysis

Various fin designs were considered. After trying various combinations of shapes, heights, sweep angles, it was determined that a symmetrical trapezoidal fin design would produce the best aerodynamic results with the best chance of the fin being intact upon landing. Other fin designs that stood out most were the tapered swept and the clipped delta.

Clipped delta wings gave us a stability of 1.56 cal:

Tapered swept wings gave us a stability of 1.66 cal:

After that we did some experimentation with a trapezoidal fin shape. This gave us a stability of 1.53 but also it is important to note that trapezoidal fin shapes are relatively flutter resistant.

Here is a diagram that gives an overview of the different fin designs available:

Each fin has a shoulder extension which is epoxied into slits in the outer tube.

Nose cone

We placed different masses in the nose cone to experiment between stability and apogee. We also experimented with effects of various nose cone shapes on apogee, spanning from ellipsoid to parabolic to conical to finally Haack series, which we used in our final design. A cavity was designed to store an avionics package, sealable from the rest of the build. We took into account that it would have to carry the avionics package, and be secured to the rest of the rocket via a shock cord and a parachute. The nose cone was modelled using CAD software.

Body

The retaining rings and centering rings take on most of the pressure of the motor during burn time. As such, two centering rings are stacked/placed adjacent to one another to provide support for the motor ring. These centering rings are what the motor will push on during the thrust phase. Two stacked centering rings will provide the support for the engine to push the rocket up. To hold the engine in during the deployment charge detonation, multiple z-clips can be screwed into the centering rings at the very bottom of the rocket to provide retention.

Final product

We had a successful launch and retrieved our rocket in perfect condition. This means that we received our level 1 build certification.

Picosatellite

The main theme for the creation of our payload was retrievability. Our payload's main design was a plume mechanism. This served as a visual marker for retrievability. On the firmware side, payload included a working LoRa, GPS, and sensors. GPS data was transmitted over LoRa during the whole duration of flight. At the same time, sensors are continuously polled. We used acceleration and altitude data to determine the stage of flight. Once landing is detected, the system turns on the plume mechanism in short bursts, stopping to allow cool down.

PCB testing

I took an active role in PCB bring-up and debugging, spending over two months testing, analysing, and troubleshooting our custom avionics boards. I was responsible for flashing and debugging the MSP430 microcontroller, validating hardware-software integration, and supporting multiple iterations of PCB rework and soldering.

This involved identifying faults using oscilloscopes and multimeters, implementing fixes, and verifying system performance through repeated testing of the payload and avionics subsystems. I participated in multiple rounds of PCB rework, including soldering components, repairing connections, and implementing wire modifications to resolve hardware issues and improve reliability.

We also carried out repeated functional tests of the vapour-generation payload to ensure consistent operation and mission readiness. This experience gave me significant exposure to PCB bring-up, embedded systems debugging, hardware troubleshooting, and iterative design refinement in a real-project environment.

Testing the coil mechanism

We tested coils of different sizes to determine the optimal plume output. Through this process, we also identified the ideal resistance of the nichrome wire needed to achieve a consistent plume. We also conducted volume testing of the plume solution to determine the volume required to produce a dense, stable plume. These tests allowed us to refine the design for more efficient performance.

The pluming mechanism consisted of: the fan holders, the fan, the plume solution chamber, and the nichrome coil.

Final product

IYPT – International Young Physicists Tournament

The problem:

What: Conducted research into the fluid mechanics of ink-alcohol fractal formation as part of a physics research competition.

How: Designed and carried out experiments, collected and analysed data, investigated the underlying fluid dynamics, and developed mathematical and physical explanations for the observed behaviour. Prepared technical presentations and defended findings before judges and competing teams.

Results: Developed strong experimental, analytical, and scientific communication skills while successfully presenting complex fluid mechanics concepts in a competitive international-style research environment.

Grapple Christchurch – Engineering Competition

What: Designed and developed an IoT-based flood monitoring and prediction system for an engineering competition.

How: Worked within a multidisciplinary team to develop a sensor network that collected environmental data and combined it with historical flood data to improve prediction capability. Contributed to the CAD design of a compact enclosure that could be mounted to existing infrastructure such as residential mailboxes.

Results: Produced a functional flood-monitoring concept that integrated sensors, data analysis, and product design, while gaining experience in IoT systems, CAD modelling, and engineering design for real-world applications.
